agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers. The design applies Domain-Driven Design with six bounded contexts organized around distinct business capabilities. The system uses an event-driven backbone for cross-context communication, keeping contexts loosely coupled while enabling real-time coordination. The core domains—Order Management and Delivery Logistics—receive the heaviest investment, as they contain the competitive differentiators (order orchestration, real-time driver assignment, ETA optimization). Supporting and generic domains (Identity, Payments, Notifications, Restaurant Catalog) are kept simple or delegated to third-party services.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"API Gateway","responsibility":"Single entry point for all clients (customer app, restaurant dashboard, driver app). Handles authentication, rate limiting, request routing, and protocol translation.","boundaries":"Stateless. No business logic. Routes to downstream services only."},{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Owns the full order lifecycle: cart creation, order placement, status transitions, cancellation, and refund initiation. Enforces order invariants (valid items, minimum totals, restaurant open hours).","boundaries":"Bounded to order aggregate. Does not know delivery assignment details—emits OrderConfirmed events for Delivery Service to consume."},{"name":"Restaurant Service","responsibility":"Manages restaurant profiles, menus, availability, operating hours, and menu item pricing. Provides catalog queries for customers and order validation for Order Service.","boundaries":"Owns restaurant and menu aggregates. Read-heavy, write-light. Does not process orders directly."},{"name":"Delivery Service","responsibility":"Handles driver registration, real-time location tracking, delivery assignment (matching orders to nearby available drivers), route optimization, and ETA calculation.","boundaries":"Owns delivery assignment and driver aggregates. Consumes OrderConfirmed events. Emits DeliveryStatusChanged events."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Processes payments, holds pre-authorizations, captures charges on delivery completion, handles refunds, and manages driver/restaurant payouts.","boundaries":"Anti-corruption layer around third-party payment provider (Stripe/Adyen). Never exposes provider internals. Consumes OrderConfirmed and DeliveryCompleted events."},{"name":"Identity Service","responsibility":"User registration, authentication (JWT issuance), role management (customer, driver, restaurant admin), and profile management.","boundaries":"Generic subdomain. Provides tokens consumed by API Gateway. No business domain logic."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends push notifications, SMS, and emails triggered by domain events (order confirmed, driver assigned, delivery arriving, etc.).","boundaries":"Pure consumer of domain events. No domain state of its own. Owns notification templates and delivery channel preferences."},{"name":"Event Bus","responsibility":"Asynchronous message broker enabling publish-subscribe communication between bounded contexts.","boundaries":"Infrastructure concern. Guarantees at-least-once delivery. Contexts must be idempotent consumers."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Order Service","contract":"REST/gRPC — CreateOrder, GetOrder, CancelOrder, ListOrders","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Restaurant Service","contract":"REST/gRPC — SearchRestaurants, GetMenu, UpdateMenu (admin)","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Delivery Service","contract":"REST/gRPC — UpdateDriverLocation (WebSocket for real-time), GetDeliveryStatus, AcceptDelivery","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"API Gateway","to":"Identity Service","contract":"REST — Register, Login, RefreshToken, GetProfile","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Restaurant Service","contract":"Synchronous query — ValidateMenuItems, CheckRestaurantOpen. Acceptable sync coupling since order placement requires immediate validation.","coupling_type":"runtime"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed, OrderCancelled","coupling_type":"event (async)"},{"from":"Delivery Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Publishes: DriverAssigned, DeliveryPickedUp, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed. Consumes: OrderConfirmed","coupling_type":"event (async)"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Consumes: OrderConfirmed (pre-auth), DeliveryCompleted (capture). Publishes: PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed, RefundIssued","coupling_type":"event (async)"},{"from":"Notification Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Consumes: OrderConfirmed, DriverAssigned, DeliveryPickedUp, DeliveryCompleted, PaymentCaptured","coupling_type":"event (async)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Event Bus","contract":"Consumes: PaymentFailed (triggers order cancellation), DeliveryCompleted (marks order delivered)","coupling_type":"event (async)"}],"data_flow":"1. Customer searches restaurants → API Gateway → Restaurant Service (read from cache/DB). 2. Customer places order → Order Service validates items with Restaurant Service synchronously → Order Service persists order → emits OrderPlaced. 3. Payment Service consumes OrderPlaced → pre-authorizes payment → emits PaymentAuthorized (or PaymentFailed). 4. Order Service consumes PaymentAuthorized → transitions to Confirmed → emits OrderConfirmed. 5. Delivery Service consumes OrderConfirmed → finds nearest available driver → assigns delivery → emits DriverAssigned. 6. Driver picks up → Delivery Service emits DeliveryPickedUp. 7. Driver delivers → Delivery Service emits DeliveryCompleted. 8. Payment Service consumes DeliveryCompleted → captures payment → emits PaymentCaptured. 9. Notification Service reacts to each event to notify relevant parties in real-time.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture","rationale":"Decouples bounded contexts. The order lifecycle spans multiple services (order → payment → delivery → notification); events allow each to evolve independently without temporal coupling."},{"pattern":"Saga (Choreography-based)","rationale":"The order placement flow (order → payment auth → confirm → assign driver) is a distributed transaction. Choreography-based saga using domain events avoids a central orchestrator and keeps contexts autonomous. Compensating actions (cancel order, release payment hold) handle failures."},{"pattern":"CQRS (within Order and Restaurant services)","rationale":"Order Service has complex write logic but simple read patterns. Restaurant Service is read-heavy (menu browsing) with infrequent writes. Separating read/write models within each service optimizes both paths without premature complexity."},{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer","rationale":"Payment Service wraps third-party payment APIs behind a domain-aligned interface, preventing vendor lock-in and model pollution."},{"pattern":"Aggregate Pattern","rationale":"Each bounded context defines aggregates as consistency boundaries: Order (with OrderItems), Restaurant (with Menu/MenuItems), DeliveryAssignment (with route), ensuring invariants are enforced transactionally."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing) so downstream services stay focused on business logic."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (evolution path)","rationale":"Start as a modular monolith with clear context boundaries. Extract to microservices only when scale demands it. The event-driven interfaces make extraction straightforward."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId; entities: OrderItem; values: Money, Address, OrderStatus; invariants: minimum order total, valid restaurant, valid status transitions)","Cart (root: CartId; values: CartItem, Quantity; invariants: items from single restaurant only)"]},{"name":"Delivery & Logistics","classification":"core","aggregates":["DeliveryAssignment (root: DeliveryId; values: PickupLocation, DropoffLocation, ETA, DeliveryStatus; invariants: one active delivery per driver, valid status transitions)","Driver (root: DriverId; values: Location, AvailabilityStatus, VehicleType; invariants: must be verified to accept deliveries)"]},{"name":"Restaurant Catalog","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Restaurant (root: RestaurantId; entities: Menu, MenuItem; values: OperatingHours, Address, CuisineType; invariants: menu items must have positive price, operating hours must be valid ranges)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: TransactionId; values: Money, PaymentMethod, TransactionStatus; invariants: capture cannot exceed authorization amount, refund cannot exceed captured amount)","Payout (root: PayoutId; values: Money, PayoutSchedule, RecipientType; invariants: payout only for completed deliveries)"]},{"name":"Identity & Access","classification":"generic","aggregates":["User (root: UserId; values: Email, PhoneNumber, Role; invariants: unique email, verified phone for drivers)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationPreference (root: UserId; values: Channel, Frequency; invariants: at least one active channel)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Delivery & Logistics","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via domain events. Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed; Delivery consumes it. Order Management does not depend on Delivery internals."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via domain events. Payments reacts to order events for authorization and capture."},{"upstream":"Restaurant Catalog","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Conformist. Order Management queries Restaurant Catalog to validate items and prices. Order Management conforms to the restaurant's menu model."},{"upstream":"Delivery & Logistics","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via domain events. Order Management consumes DeliveryCompleted to finalize order status."},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via domain events. Order Management consumes PaymentAuthorized/PaymentFailed to confirm or cancel orders."},{"upstream":"All Contexts","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Open Host Service / Published Language. All contexts publish events in a shared event schema. Notifications subscribes as a downstream consumer with no upstream influence."},{"upstream":"Identity & Access","downstream":"All Contexts","relationship":"Shared Kernel (minimal). User identity (UserId, Role) is shared across contexts. Kept deliberately thin—only ID and role, no profile data leakage."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Choreography-based saga over orchestration-based saga","gains":"Each service is autonomous, no single point of failure for the saga coordinator, simpler to reason about within each context","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize the full order lifecycle, compensating actions must be carefully designed in each service, debugging distributed flows requires good observability","alternatives_considered":["Orchestration saga with a central Order Saga Coordinator — simpler to trace but creates coupling and a bottleneck","Two-phase commit — too slow and fragile for a real-time delivery platform"]},{"decision":"Start as modular monolith, extract later","gains":"Faster initial delivery, simpler deployment and debugging, easier refactoring of context boundaries before they're hardened into service boundaries","sacrifices":"Requires discipline to maintain module boundaries in a single deployable, risk of shortcuts that couple modules","alternatives_considered":["Microservices from day one — higher operational overhead, premature for an unproven product","Serverless functions per endpoint — too granular, cold start issues for real-time features"]},{"decision":"Synchronous call from Order Service to Restaurant Service for menu validation","gains":"Immediate consistency — orders cannot be placed with invalid items or closed restaurants","sacrifices":"Runtime coupling between two services; Restaurant Service downtime blocks order placement","alternatives_considered":["Cache menu data in Order Service — reduces coupling but risks stale prices or unavailable items","Validate asynchronously and compensate — poor UX, customer sees order accepted then cancelled"]},{"decision":"Event bus with at-least-once delivery (not exactly-once)","gains":"Simpler infrastructure, higher throughput, well-supported by commodity brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS)","sacrifices":"All consumers must implement idempotency (using event IDs / idempotency keys)","alternatives_considered":["Exactly-once semantics — complex, limited broker support, marginal benefit if consumers are idempotent"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Provider","evolution_path":"Anti-corruption layer means swapping Stripe for Adyen (or adding multiple providers) requires changes only within the Payment Service boundary."},{"area":"Delivery Matching Algorithm","evolution_path":"Driver assignment logic is encapsulated in Delivery Service. Can evolve from simple nearest-driver to ML-based optimization without affecting other contexts."},{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) only requires changes in Notification Service. Event schema remains stable."},{"area":"Service Extraction","evolution_path":"Modular monolith boundaries mirror future microservice boundaries. Extract any context to its own deployable when scale justifies it. Event-driven interfaces require no protocol change."},{"area":"Restaurant Onboarding","evolution_path":"Restaurant Catalog is a supporting domain. Can be replaced with a third-party restaurant management SaaS or enhanced with self-service onboarding without touching core domains."},{"area":"Real-time Tracking","evolution_path":"WebSocket connections for driver location can evolve to Server-Sent Events or gRPC streaming. Isolated behind the API Gateway and Delivery Service boundary."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order State Machine","reason":"Order status transitions (Placed → PaymentAuthorized → Confirmed → PickedUp → Delivered / Cancelled) must be strictly enforced. Invalid transitions corrupt business state. This is intentionally rigid."},{"area":"Payment Authorization → Capture Flow","reason":"Financial operations must follow a strict authorize-then-capture lifecycle. Deviations risk overcharging or revenue leakage. Rigidity is a feature here."},{"area":"Event Schema Contracts","reason":"Published event schemas are contracts between contexts. Breaking changes require versioning and consumer migration. Treat as public APIs."},{"area":"Single-Restaurant Cart Constraint","reason":"Delivery logistics and restaurant preparation become exponentially complex with multi-restaurant orders. Constraining carts to a single restaurant is a deliberate simplification that should only be relaxed with significant architectural investment."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Saga failure leaves order in inconsistent state (e.g., payment captured but delivery never assigned)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Implement dead-letter queues for failed events, build a saga state view (read model) for monitoring stuck orders, add automated and manual compensation workflows, alert on orders stuck in intermediate states >N minutes."},{"risk":"Delivery Service becomes bottleneck during peak hours (real-time location updates + assignment algorithm)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Separate location ingestion (high-write, append-only) from assignment logic (compute-intensive, periodic). Use geospatial indexing (PostGIS / Redis GEO) for efficient proximity queries. Horizontally scale location ingestion independently."},{"risk":"Restaurant Service downtime blocks all order placement due to synchronous validation","severity":"high","mitigation":"Cache recent menu data in Order Service with short TTL (5 min). Fall back to cached data with a warning. Implement circuit breaker pattern on the sync call."},{"risk":"Event ordering issues cause incorrect state transitions (e.g., DeliveryCompleted processed before DriverAssigned)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use partition keys (OrderId) to guarantee ordering per order within the event bus. Consumers should validate preconditions before applying events and retry/requeue if preconditions aren't met."},{"risk":"Driver location data privacy and regulatory compliance (GDPR, local regulations)","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Store location data with retention policies. Anonymize historical data. Ensure location is only shared with relevant parties (customer sees driver during active delivery only). Build consent management into Identity Service."},{"risk":"Multi-region expansion introduces data residency and latency challenges","severity":"low","mitigation":"Design for region-aware deployment from the start: region field on Restaurant and Driver aggregates, event bus supports regional topics. Defer implementation until expansion is imminent."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1: Build as a modular monolith in a single repository. Each bounded context is a top-level module with its own domain model, application services, and persistence. Modules communicate via an in-process event bus (easily swapped for Kafka/RabbitMQ later). Phase 2: Add real-time features (driver tracking via WebSockets, push notifications). Phase 3: Extract Delivery Service first (highest independent scaling need), then Payment Service (regulatory isolation). Keep Order, Restaurant, and Identity co-deployed until traffic justifies separation.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/order/ — Order context: domain model, application services, event publishers, REST controllers","src/delivery/ — Delivery context: domain model, driver matching, location tracking, event consumers/publishers","src/restaurant/ — Restaurant context: catalog, menu management, availability","src/payment/ — Payment context: ACL over payment provider, transaction management","src/identity/ — Identity context: auth, user management, JWT","src/notification/ — Notification context: event consumers, channel dispatchers, templates","src/shared/events/ — Published event schemas (shared kernel, versioned)","src/shared/types/ — Shared value objects (Money, Address, UserId — kept minimal)","src/infrastructure/eventbus/ — Event bus abstraction (in-process for Phase 1, broker-backed for Phase 3)","src/infrastructure/gateway/ — API Gateway configuration, routing, auth middleware"],"patterns_to_follow":"Hexagonal architecture within each module: domain at the center (no framework dependencies), ports define interfaces, adapters implement infrastructure. Use factory methods on aggregate roots for creation. Emit domain events from aggregates, dispatch after persistence (transactional outbox pattern for reliability). Repository pattern for all persistence — no direct DB access from application services.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Sharing database tables across bounded contexts — each context owns its schema","Leaking domain objects across context boundaries — use DTOs or events at boundaries","Building a distributed monolith — if you extract services but they all fail together, you've gained complexity without resilience","Over-engineering the delivery matching algorithm early — start with nearest-available-driver, optimize with data later","Skipping the transactional outbox — publishing events outside the DB transaction leads to lost events or ghost events","Putting business logic in API controllers or event handlers — keep it in the domain layer"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Bounded context boundaries — these align with natural business capabilities and team ownership","Event-driven integration pattern — well-proven for multi-party coordination platforms","Order and payment lifecycle — standard patterns with well-understood failure modes","Modular monolith first approach — right trade-off for a greenfield platform"],"low_confidence_areas":["Delivery matching algorithm specifics — depends heavily on geography, driver density, and operational constraints not yet defined","Exact event bus technology choice — depends on team expertise and infrastructure budget (Kafka vs RabbitMQ vs managed cloud service)","Multi-restaurant order support timeline — business decision that significantly impacts architecture if introduced later","Real-time scaling characteristics — need load testing data to validate WebSocket and location tracking design"],"assumptions":["Single-region deployment initially","Restaurant count in hundreds to low thousands at launch","Driver fleet in hundreds initially, scaling to thousands","Mobile-first customer experience with web as secondary","Team size of 4-8 engineers (influences modular monolith recommendation)","Third-party payment processor (not building payments infrastructure)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a food delivery platform connecting restaurants, customers, and delivery drivers. The design applies Domain-Driven Design with six bounded contexts organized around distinct business capabilities. The system uses an event-driven backbone for cross-context communication, keeping contexts loosely coupled while enabling real-time coordination.
